metadata.title:
THE BIGGEST QUESTION


NOW BEFORE CONGRESS

GOVERNMENT CONTROL OF FREIGHT RATES
WILL CAUSE A BITTER FIGHT-CONGRESS-


MAN COOPER S REMEDY.

pages.raw_text:
30 days of the filing of the order by
the commission. While the appeal
was pending the order of the COM
mission and the rate fixed by it would
stand until set aside by the court.
The bill also provides for appeal from
the circuit court to the supreme court
of the United States.


I'm order to expedite hearings on
these cases they are to be given pre-
cedence over all other business ex-
cept criminal cases, so that there
need be no delay in reaching a final
adjudication.


1.-The shipper is to be given the
same right of appeal as is granted
the railroad"


With regard to the effect of the
bill and the need for such regula-
ton Cooper said that it was not an
attack upon the railroads mo sensi-
BILE man. he continued, has any SYM
pathy with professional agitators who
denounce railroads and railroad man.
ayers Unfortunately, but it is a
fact not to be denied that shippers
are often grossly wronged through
railroad practices for which today
there is no remedy"


He said that railroad rates were
essentially tax which shippers were
| obliged to pay and that as they were
levied to raise dividends it was natur-
al that there should be some abuse.
He stated that the interstate COM
| merce commission had the right to
enforce proper conditions. The re-
suit was that a man forced to pay


an unjust rate had practically no


hope of redress.


| Replying to a criticism made by


some railroad managers, Cooper as


serted that the interstate commerce


commission could not violently disar-


range the railroad tariffs of the coun-


try, but under the bill it could In an


orderly way adjust those that were


grossly unfair


| In summing up Cooper repeated a


ago to this effect:


| 1.-The railroad business is monop-
oily of transportation, and, more


over, monopoly which the shippers


of the country are bound to use. AS


such monopoly the railroad business


has power to levy a tax on every


other business Ought this power to


be unregulated? In my Judgment this


constitutes one of the supremely inn


portant questions before the Ameri.


can people,"


-Four days after the introduction OF


the Cooper bill in the house a dupli.


cate bill was presented by Senator
Quarles of Wisconsin in the senate


Both are now in the hands of COM


mittees.
